entry.title: Re: Position of Equipment in Asset Viewer/Overview
entry.content:
Hello

I don't have any experience with the "Asset Overview" but you can always consider the re-purposing of either the "Sort Field" or the Classification System ... I would opt to use the "Sort Field" with some input like a "<FuncLoc>_<Position>" value or something, so you can atleast SORT via "IE05" and also use it as a SearchFilter in the "Display TechObj App" ...

Alternatively, you could set up a Classification / Characteristics system that basically does the same. The Classification option can also work for the Equipment-Lists Applications, but is probably more intense in terms of maintenance and thougher as a datasource when you engage in Development / Reporting (via CDS etc.) ...

Both options have Mass-Update possibilities for an initial upload and overall Maintenance ("IE05" Mass-changes enablement of "Sort Field", Trx. "CLMM" for Classification etc.). 

In absence of more expert opinions on the Asset-Overview Solution, that would be my suggestion ...

Hope this helps

Nic T.

entry.title: Re: Hello SAP Community
entry.content:
Hi Purnima, welcome to the community! 👋


Great to see someone jumping into SAP FICO with such a clear focus and genuine enthusiasm. Working on a real implementation project from the start is honestly the best learning path — no course gives you what hands-on configuration with real business requirements does.
GL, AP, AR, and Asset Accounting are the core pillars of the module. Once you have a solid grasp of those, everything else starts to click into place.

Feel free to reach out whenever you have questions — happy to help whenever I can! 🙌
Also, make sure to check if your city or country has a SAP Community Location Group — it's a great way to connect with people nearby who are also working with SAP. You might find local peers, mentors, or even job opportunities closer than you think!

Keep learning and don't hesitate to ask. Wishing you a lot of success on the project! 🚀

entry.title: Clarification on using Object grouping in Real Estate Contracts - S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi CLM Experts,

We would like to seek clarification on how to properly leverage Object Grouping in Real Estate Contracts within S/4HANA Public Cloud.
In our current business scenario, a tenant may lease multiple units under a single contract. For example, a tenant may lease 20 units, but these units may have different unit prices depending on the group.

Sample scenario:
• 10 units → Unit price: 2,000
• 10 units → Unit price: 2,500

Our intention is to group the leased objects so that:
• A single condition with one unit price can be applied per object group.
• The system can calculate the total amount based on the quantity of objects within each group.

We would like to clarify the following:
1. How can Object Grouping be utilized in S/4HANA Public Cloud Real Estate Contracts to support this requirement?
2. Is there a standard approach to group multiple rental objects and assign a single rate per group?
3. If object grouping is supported, could you advise on the recommended configuration or process to achieve this scenario?

Thank you.

entry.title: Two Ways to Build Agentic Reporting in SAP: Python Agent on SAP BTP vs. Joule Studio Agent
entry.content:
1. Introduction

In my previous blog post on Agentic Reporting, I talked about how modern reporting goes beyond dashboards — it requires systems that reason, explain, and act.

In this follow‑up, instead of going straight into a specific implementation, I want to answer a bigger question:

What are the different ways to build an AI Agent in the SAP ecosystem?

With SAP’s evolving AI landscape, you now have two powerful paths to build enterprise‑grade agents:

Option A — Build a Python Agent on SAP BTP

Custom, full‑code, flexible Agent service that integrates with SAP Datasphere via SQL or OData.

Option B — Build an Agent directly in SAP Joule Studio

A no‑code/low‑code agent builder inside SAP Build for reasoning, orchestration, and multi‑system actions.

Both options are valid. Both can support Agentic Reporting. But they differ significantly in architecture, flexibility, governance, and effort.

This post compares both, so you can choose the best path for your use case.

2. The Two Agent‑Building Paths Explained

Option A — Python Agent on SAP BTP (Full‑Code + Maximum Flexibility)

This is the classic approach where you build an application in Python, deploy it on SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry or Kyma), and connect it to SAP Datasphere.

When is this ideal?

  • Heavy data processing
  • Custom ML/AI logic
  • Integration with complex non-SAP systems
  • You want maximum control over logic, dependencies, libraries
  • Advanced reasoning models + custom frameworks
  • You want to host an API that other tools can call (including Joule)

What your Python Agent can do

  • Connect to SAP Datasphere using:
    • SQL (hdbcli)
    • OData Analytical or Relational APIs
  • Execute complex logic (anomaly detection, simulations, scoring)
  • Write data back to Open SQL schema
  • Expose REST endpoints for other SAP systems and Joule agents to consume

Architecture Summary

  • Python app on BTP
  • Service bindings → HANA Cloud credentials via VCAP_SERVICES
  • OAuth → Datasphere OData access
  • HANA Cloud SQL → read/write via Open SQL schema
  • Python performs reasoning, actions, decisions

Option B — Joule Studio Agent (SAP-native no‑code Agent Builder)

SAP Joule Studio allows business teams and architects to build agents without needing to write any code.

These agents can:

  • Plan
  • Reason
  • Execute workflows
  • Call APIs (via Joule Skills)
  • Use SAP Knowledge Graph
  • Use Document Grounding
  • Orchestrate SAP and non‑SAP tools

When is this ideal?

  • You want an SAP-native, governed agent
  • You want quick setup (no DevOps, no runtime)
  • You want conversational + actionable agents
  • You want orchestration across SAP applications
  • You want to avoid managing Python runtimes
  • You want automatic reasoning and traceability

What your Joule Agent can do

  • Connect to SAP systems via destinations
  • Invoke Joule Skills (API calls, BTP actions)
  • Consume Datasphere data via OData APIs
  • Perform reasoning using SAP’s LLMs
  • Automate workflows in SuccessFactors, S/4HANA, Ariba, etc.

Architecture Summary

  • Joule Studio = Agent Builder
  • Uses Destinations for integration
  • Uses Skills & Actions for API calls
  • LLM-powered agentic reasoning

No deploy, no runtime maintenance

3. Comparing the Two Approaches

4. Which Option Should You Use?

When to Choose Python Agent on BTP

Technical Complexity Requirements:

  • Advanced algorithmic logic - When you need custom algorithms beyond standard business logic
  • Heavy transformations/ETL - For complex data processing workflows requiring full programmatic control 
  • External ML libraries - Integration with sklearn, PyTorch, transformers, or other specialized libraries 

Integration Scenarios:

  • API endpoint creation - When other systems need to call your agent as a service
  • Any system connectivity - Leveraging the "any API, any system" capability for non-SAP integrations 

When to Choose Joule Studio Agent

Rapid Development Needs:

  • Fast deployment with the "lower effort, no-code" approach 
  • Built-in agentic reasoning eliminating custom development overhead 

Enterprise Governance:

  • SAP-native governance & logging for compliance and auditability 
  • Built-in trace, executions, reasoning view for operational transparency 
  • Skill marketplace access for pre-built capabilities 

SAP Ecosystem Integration:

  • SAP-first integrations with S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, and Ariba 
  • SAP Knowledge Graph + Documents for automatic grounding 
  • Workflow automation & orchestration as the primary use case

5. Conclusion:

SAP’s Agentic AI is evolving rapidly, and you now have two mature ways of building enterprise‑grade agents:

1. Python Agent on SAP BTP

Flexible, powerful, code-based, perfect for analytics and complex logic.

2️. Joule Studio Agent

SAP-native, governed, no-code, perfect for orchestration, reasoning, and functional automation.

Both are valid choices and can even work together.

 

Next in the series:

  • Blog #3: How to Build a Python Agent for Agentic Reporting (Datasphere + BTP)
  • Blog #4: How to Build an SAP-native Joule Agent for Agentic Reporting

Previous Blog in the series:

Blog #1: Agentic Reporting - The Next Evolution of SAP Reporting

entry.title: Clarification on Automatic Payment Block After Reset Cleared Items in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We have a requirement related to the behavior of the Reset Cleared Items functionality in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

1. Current System Behavior

Currently, when a user performs the Reverse and Reset action using the Reset Cleared Items app, the system reverses the payment document and the associated invoice becomes open again.

2. Expected Functionality

Once the payment is reversed and reset, the invoice that becomes open should be automatically blocked for payment.

3. Clarification

Is there any standard functionality in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud that allows the system to automatically apply a payment block on the invoice after the Reverse and Reset action is executed?

4. Alternative Requirement (if the above is not possible)

If automatic blocking is not available in the standard functionality, we would like to know whether it is possible to configure a notification or alert that triggers immediately after the reversal/reset action.

The notification should:

  • Be sent to a specific user or user group

  • Contain details of the reversed payment document

  • Include the invoice number that has become open again

  • Inform the responsible user so they can manually apply a payment block on the invoice

5. Request for Guidance

Could you please confirm whether this requirement can be achieved through:

  • Standard configuration

  • Extensibility options available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

 

Thanks and Regards,

Rohini Dutta

entry.title: Re: New Pipeline Concept features: integrated runtimes and supporting landscape stages
entry.content:
Hi Alex,

when processing an real big EO message the iFow "Pipeline v2 Generic Step01 - Inbound Processing for XI" failed with error 

org.apache.cxf.interceptor.Fault: Could not generate the XML stream caused by: javax.xml.stream.XMLStreamException: Text size limit (134217728) exceeded., cause: javax.xml.stream.XMLStreamException: Text size limit (134217728) exceeded'

It happened at the the end of the iFlow. It seems, that it had problems to parse the response of the JMS-Adapter back to the XI response, which should not be needed in an asynchronous scenario. I was not able to reproduce the problem on dev.
 
But I think such an approach can help, and would decrease the exchanged message load:

I would appreciate, if you would adjust the packaged iFlow.

Thanks
Bernd

entry.title: Re: Clean Core maturity and the new Extensibility Levels
entry.content:
@rajeshps using IDocs with extensions often ends up closer to Level C, since IDocs are a more classic integration approach and extensions may depend on non‑released objects (depending on the S/4HANA Cloud edition and what is actually released). For Clean Core Level B, prefer released/stable SAP APIs (OData/SOAP) and/or event-based integration. To minimize middleware latency, look at asynchronous/event-driven patterns over tight synchronous calls

have in mind that RAP/ABAP Cloud is the strategic direction, but Clean Core alignment mainly depends on using released extensibility and APIs

entry.title: Bringing Analytics to Life with Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst
entry.content:
For more than 15 years, Université de Sherbrooke has been a member of the SAP University Alliance, and our School of Management has continuously benefited from this global academic community to innovate in the teaching of enterprise systems. Over the years, SAP technologies have allowed us to move beyond theory and immerse students in realistic, hands-on learning experiences. With the launch of Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst, this pedagogical journey has taken an exciting new turn, one that immediately resonated with our students.

 

From the classroom to the rink: a natural fit

When we first introduced Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst in class, one of the most striking aspects was how easy it was to deploy. As a free, cloud-based solution built on SAP Analytics Cloud, the game required no complex setup, no local installations, and no steep learning curve. Within a single class session, students were able to log in, explore dashboards, and begin analyzing data. This simplicity made it easy to integrate the game seamlessly into existing courses.

The hockey context immediately grabbed attention. Even students who were not die-hard fans quickly became engaged, as the scenario provided a concrete and intuitive way to understand abstract analytics concepts. The connection between data, performance metrics, and decision-making became tangible almost instantly.

 

Student reactions: curiosity turns into engagement

The reaction from students was overwhelmingly positive. What began as curiosity (“We’re analyzing NHL data in class?”) quickly turned into deep engagement. The competitive and playful nature of the game encouraged participation, discussion, and collaboration. Perhaps most importantly, the game lowered the intimidation factor often associated with analytics. Students who initially felt less confident with data became more comfortable experimenting, exploring visualizations, and asking questions. The sports narrative created a safe and motivating environment for learning.

 

What students actually learned

Beyond engagement, the learning outcomes were clear. Students developed core analytics competencies, including data interpretation, KPI analysis, and storytelling with dashboards. Because everything is grounded in SAP Analytics Cloud, students also gained hands-on experience with a professional-grade analytics platform used by thousands of organizations worldwide. This connection between academic learning and real business tools significantly enhanced the perceived relevance of the activity.

The availability of multiple versions (novice and advanced), with different gameplay scenarios (goal scoring, goalies) also allowed us to adapt the experience to different levels, from introductory analytics courses to more advanced classes.

 

Advice for fellow educators

For educators considering Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst, my main advice is simple: start small and let the game do the work. You don’t need to redesign an entire course to benefit from it. Even a short in-class activity can spark curiosity and meaningful discussions. Encourage students to explore, make mistakes, and reflect on their decisions rather than focusing solely on “winning” the game.

Finally, embrace the storytelling aspect. The real power of this approach lies in how it connects data, technology, and human decision-making within a narrative students care about.

 

A powerful addition to analytics education

Business Builders – The Hockey Analyst represents a compelling example of how partnerships between academia, industry, and sports organizations (SAP, the NHL, and the ERPsim Lab at HEC Montréal) can produce innovative, engaging, and pedagogically sound learning experiences. For us at Université de Sherbrooke, it has become a valuable tool to enrich analytics education, spark student motivation, and bridge the gap between classroom learning and real-world practice.

-Prof. Olivier Caya, Université de Sherbrooke (Canada)

entry.title: Building an AI Recruiting Agent with SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Extending SmartRecruiters with predictive intelligence — helping recruiters act before candidates disengage.

SmartRecruiters already provides powerful tools for sourcing, tracking, and hiring candidates. But now you can add a predictive layer — a custom agent that proactively surfaces issues before they become problems.

What we built

The SmartRecruiters custom AI Recruiting Agent does three things:

1. Spots bottlenecks. It compares how long candidates sit in each stage against benchmarks. When someone's been in "Interview" for 18 days (benchmark is 7), the agent flags it, identifies likely causes, and suggests next steps.

🚨 CRITICAL: Senior Software Engineer - Interview Stage
   6 candidates stuck avg 18 days (benchmark: 7)
   Root cause: Geographic scheduling delays
   → Schedule batch video interviews this week

2. Predicts disengagement risk. A senior engineer sitting in your pipeline for two weeks likely has other opportunities. The agent scores each candidate's risk based on inactivity, seniority, and stage — then tells you who to prioritize today.

3. Forecasts timelines. Using conversion rates (50% of interviews become offers, 85% of offers get accepted), it predicts when you'll actually fill each role. Data-driven answers instead of estimates.

 

Extending SmartRecruiters

SmartRecruiters is a leading talent acquisition platform providing:

  • Attract — Career sites, sourcing, CRM, job distribution
  • Select — ATS, AI matching, screening, scheduling
  • Hire — Analytics, offer management, onboarding

This agent adds a predictive intelligence layer on top of SmartRecruiters data:

SmartRecruiters provides This agent adds

Candidate trackingBottleneck detection with root causes
Pipeline visibilityDisengagement risk prediction
Hiring analyticsTimeline forecasting with confidence
Dashboard reportingProactive Teams alerts

Think of it as an early warning system built on your existing SmartRecruiters data.

Why an agent, not a dashboard?

Dashboards are great for exploration, but recruiters are busy — back-to-back calls, juggling multiple open roles, responding to messages. They need insights that come to them at the right moment.

So we built an agent that:

  • Pushes alerts to Teams when something needs attention
  • Answers questions in plain language ("who's at risk?")
  • Explains why — not just "6 candidates stuck" but "scheduling delays in APAC region"
  • Suggests actions — not just problems, but what to do about them

The tech stack

Component Technology Role

LLMSAP AI Core (gpt-4o-mini)Natural language understanding and response generation
Agent PatternReAct (Reasoning + Acting)Structured decision-making loop
FrameworkLangChain + LangGraphTool orchestration and agent workflow
Data SourceSmartRecruiters APIReal-time candidate and job data
BackendFastAPIAsync REST API serving the UI and Teams
NotificationsMicrosoft Teams Adaptive CardsProactive alerts via webhooks

The whole thing is about 1,000 lines of Python. No complex infrastructure, no Kubernetes, no microservices.

APIs vs LLMs: Who does what?

This is important: the LLM doesn't do the analysis. Here's how we split responsibilities:

Task Handled by Why

Fetch candidates and jobsSmartRecruiters APISource of truth for recruiting data
Calculate bottleneck severityPython codeDeterministic, testable, fast
Score candidate riskPython codeConsistent scoring rules
Forecast hiring timelinesPython codeMath-based conversion modeling
Understand user questionsSAP AI Core LLMNatural language is messy
Generate human-readable responsesSAP AI Core LLMMakes data conversational
Send alerts to TeamsTeams Webhook APIDirect HTTP POST

The LLM is the "voice" of the agent — it interprets what you're asking and explains results in plain language. But the actual analysis happens in deterministic Python functions. This keeps results consistent and explainable.

The ReAct pattern

ReAct stands for Reasoning + Acting. It's a loop where the agent:

  1. Thinks — "The user asked about bottlenecks. I should call the bottleneck analysis tool."
  2. Acts — Calls the analyze_bottlenecks() function
  3. Observes — Gets back structured data (jobs, stages, days stuck, severity)
  4. Thinks again — "I have the data. Now I should explain this clearly."
  5. Responds — Uses the LLM to generate a natural language summary
User: "Show me bottlenecks"
         ↓
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │  THINK          │  → "User wants bottleneck analysis"
    └────────┬────────┘
             ↓
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │  ACT            │  → Call analyze_bottlenecks()
    └────────┬────────┘
             ↓
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │  OBSERVE        │  → Get structured results from API + analysis
    └────────┬────────┘
             ↓
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │  RESPOND        │  → LLM synthesizes into readable answer
    └─────────────────┘

Why ReAct instead of just prompting the LLM with all the data? Control. We decide which tools to call and when. The LLM handles language, not logic.

How the agent processes a request

When you ask "who's at risk?", here's the full flow:

1. USER INPUT
   "Who's at risk of dropping out?"
              ↓
2. INTENT DETECTION
   Keywords: "risk", "dropping" → Route to risk analysis
              ↓
3. API CALL (SmartRecruiters)
   GET /candidates → Returns 100 candidates with status, stage, timestamps
              ↓
4. ANALYSIS (Python)
   For each candidate:
   - Days inactive? → +20 or +40 points
   - Senior in early stage? → +25 points
   - Offer pending too long? → +30 points
   → Returns ranked list with scores
              ↓
5. LLM SYNTHESIS (SAP AI Core)
   Prompt: "Summarize these at-risk candidates for a recruiter..."
   → Returns conversational response
              ↓
6. RESPONSE TO USER
   "You have 3 candidates who need immediate attention..."

Fallback mode

If SAP AI Core is temporarily unavailable, the agent doesn't break. It falls back to:

  1. Keyword-based intent detection — still routes to the right analysis
  2. Template responses — pre-formatted summaries of the data
  3. Full functionality — all the analysis still works, just less conversational

This means the agent is always useful, even without LLM access.

The analysis under the hood

Bottleneck detection

We set benchmarks for each stage:

  • In Review: 5 days
  • Interview: 7 days
  • Offer: 5 days

If the average time exceeds 1.5x the benchmark, it's moderate. Over 2.5x? Critical.

The agent also identifies likely causes. Interview delays in a job with candidates across multiple countries? Probably scheduling across time zones. Long review times? The hiring manager may need a nudge.

Risk scoring

We add up points:

  • Inactive over 14 days: +40
  • Inactive over 7 days: +20
  • Senior candidate (5+ years) in early stage: +25
  • Offer pending: +30
  • Offer pending over 5 days: +15
  • In Interview over 10 days: +20

Scores over 50 mean "reach out this week." Over 80? Prioritize them today.

Timeline forecasting

Simple funnel math:

  • 85% of offers become hires
  • 50% of interviews become offers
  • 40% of reviews become interviews

Multiply through your current pipeline and you get predicted hires. Compare against openings to see if you're on track.

Pushing to Teams

The best feature might be the Teams integration. Instead of recruiters checking the agent, the agent checks on them.

We built four card types:

  • Daily digest — summary of everything, with an AI-written executive summary
  • Bottleneck alerts — only fires when there's something critical
  • Risk alerts — only when candidates need immediate attention
  • Forecast updates — weekly "here's where we stand"

Set up an Incoming Webhook in Teams, add the URL to your config, run python scheduler.py digest, and you're done.

Connecting to SmartRecruiters

The SmartRecruiters API is straightforward. OAuth2 client credentials, then:

response = requests.get(
    '<https://api.smartrecruiters.com/candidates>',
    headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'},
    params={'limit': 100}
)

We cache tokens and refresh them before expiry. The agent also works with mock data for demos and development — set USE_MOCK_DATA=true and you're ready to go.

What we learned

1. Resilience matters. Building a keyword-based fallback means the agent stays useful even during connectivity issues. Always have a backup plan.

2. "Why" is more important than "what." Telling a recruiter "6 candidates are stuck" isn't helpful. Telling them "6 candidates are stuck because interviews can't be scheduled across time zones — try batch video calls" is actionable.

3. Meet people where they are. The Teams integration drove significantly more engagement than the web UI. Bring insights to where people already work.

4. Mock data is a feature. Not just for development — it's great for demos, onboarding, and showing stakeholders what the tool does without exposing real candidate data.

Try it

Everything's open source:

GitHub: https://github.tools.sap/agent-samples/smart-recruiters-agent.git

git clone <https://github.tools.sap/agent-samples/smart-recruiters-agent.git>
cd smartrecruiters-recruiting-agent
pip install -r requirements.txt
python app.py

Works out of the box with mock data. Add your SAP AI Core and SmartRecruiters credentials for the full experience.

This project started as an experiment in agentic AI for HR tech — and showed us that recruiters really value an assistant that helps them stay ahead of candidate engagement.

Links:

  • SmartRecruiters — Talent acquisition platform
  • SmartRecruiters API — Developer documentation
  • SAP AI Core — Enterprise AI infrastructure
  • LangChain — Agent framework
  • Adaptive Cards — Teams card designer

entry.title: Re: Unit of Measure for Total Capacity in Storage Bin (LS...
entry.content: The Total Capacity field has no alternative UoM.  it is always a count of quants. The only UoM you can maintain at bin level is for weight (GEWEI). For volume or other measures, you must configure capacity checks at the storage type level and maintain bin dimensions.

entry.title: Emerging Technology and Caged Thoughts
entry.content:
The boom that  we are witnessing now is louder than anything our ancestors could have  ever imagined and faster than any academician could ever track.Technology no longer evolves year by year,it now evolves month by month, week by week and now hour by hour. What once took decades now takes days. This is tremendous growth. Revolutionary. Historic.

But growth at this speed comes with a  psychological cost.

With every new model, every new breakthrough, a silent companion , imposter syndrome appears.A creeping anxiety that whispers: “You are far behind.”

As a master’s student, I constantly feel the pressure to keep up with emerging technologies. The journey no longer moves from one programming language to another. It leaps from machine learning to deep learning, from ChatGPT to Gemini, Claude, AI agents, Agentic AI and beyond.

By the time I begin understanding one trend, another replaces it.This endless tracking doesn’t always create productivity  but hinders it and comes along procrastination. A procrastination phase so deep it feels like sinking into the Mariana Trench, surrounded by information, yet unable to move.

In 2026, I thought I had found clarity: “I need a Vision Board.” Then another dilemma arises.Should I use Pinterest? A vision board app? A productivity tool? A Notion template?Humorous, that even organizing clarity now requires choosing between technologies.And that’s when I realized something important:-

The problem isn’t the growth of technology, the problem is trying to grow in every direction at once.

“If you are planning to dwell in tech, delve deeper first, then broader.”

Depth builds confidence,breadth without depth builds anxiety.Emerging technology is expanding at a humongous pace , at the same time our thoughts don’t need to be caged by it.We don’t need to learn everything, sometimes, the most revolutionary act in a fast-moving world
is choosing where to stand still.

entry.title: Re: Emerging Technology and Caged Thoughts
entry.content:
Hi @adithya_ajith ,

Agree! Sometimes "just pick a direction then go" is the best way to proceed.

You can also leverage the tech to keep up, prompting an LLM to keep you up to date with the latest in succinct bullet points is already a big help.

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Cloud (Public) – SD Delivery/PGI: use own sto...
entry.content:
No it does not split quantities automatically between own stock and consignment stock during delivery picking. Consignment (special stock K) is treated as vendor‑owned until you transfer it into your own stock. SAP requires explicit transfer posting before consumption.  This means, you need to perform a transfer posting from consignment (K) → own unrestricted stock before delivery picking.

SAP’s recommended practice is to transfer consignment stock into own stock before delivery. This ensures clean accounting (vendor liability cleared, own stock consumed) and avoids mixed consumption logic at PGI.

entry.title: Re: Picking list from outbound delivery for WM managed wa...
entry.content:
Dear @ST24,

SAP has a Note that matches your symptom exactly:

  • Line item details are not displayed in picklist output
  • Symptom no item details printed for the pick list of an EWM-relevant outbound delivery
  • Cause by design
  • Resolution print from the EWM Warehouse Task instead

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3463051

What to do instead:

1. Print the pick list from EWM (Warehouse Order / Warehouse Tasks)

Use the warehouse execution flow and print the WO/WT pick list not the delivery pick list:

  • In Process Warehouse Tasks Picking, SAP explicitly refers to printing the pick list for a warehouse order and points to controlling it via Output Parameter Determination

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/5c2fdcb218d04bac85a64169b05bb357.html

2. Set up automatic printing (recommended)

Configure output for Warehouse Order documents using Output Parameter Determination. This is the intended place to control pick list printing for warehouse operations

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/fdc3cf565b1b6b15e10000000a441470.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/3e20e815330b49b29f7972d88e0e9076.html

If you’re searching for the predelivered form template look for EWM_PICK_LIST_2 for printing the warehouse order pick list

More details below:

https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-q-a/picking-list-and-packing-list-in-s-4hana-public-cloud-ewm/qaq-p/14270773

Best Reagards,

Dawid

entry.title: WSSE error "The security token could not be authenticated or authorized" in Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Hello All,
I am currently working on an integration flow where SOAP 1.x adapter with WS-Security configuration is used in combination with a request-reply step. The request execution completes successfully but after the response generation phase the request-reply step fails with the following error - "org.apache.cxf.binding.soap.SoapFault: The security token could not be authenticated or authorized, cause: org.apache.wss4j.common.ext.WSSecurityException: The security token could not be authenticated or authorized"

I would like to understand if some kind of validation is occurring for the response payload at the SAP CPI side and is that why it is failing and that is there any mechanism to bypass this validation? Also would like to get clarity on another thing - in the response payload in the binary security token, valuetype attribute is PKIPathv1, which means the complete certificate chain is bundled together for signing purpose. But in SAP cpi we have uploaded the certificates separately as leaf, intermediate and root. Can this be a cause? 


When trace is enabled, I am able to see the SOAP response payload in the message logs. However, I would like to understand how the response payload can be captured within the exception subprocess when this failure occurs. I tried using ${in.body}, but it provided a blank body.
Has anyone encountered a similar scenario or have suggestions on how to capture the response payload in the exception subprocess?
Kindly see the below attachments for reference.

Thanks & Regards,

Aditya

entry.title: Re: Restrict revoking of TECO in CO02 for some users in S...
entry.content:
Good day @kristina_vl 

Thank you for your question.

The Public Cloud allows you to use a status profile and Business Transaction Control to block or allow business transactions at TECO. For example, BTAB for TECO and BUTA for revoke TECO.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/cb7414a774c246ee894a8619d94267fc.html?locale=en-US

However, it does not allow you to make that same status profile rule apply differently per user. Status profile logic is based on order, not on user. So, the only clean way to let “some users” revoke TECO and others not in authorisation design via business roles, not via the status profile alone.

The following references confirm that TECO related actions are executed through status processing, and revoke is an exception you can control.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/daffb753128eb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/cb7414a774c246ee894a8619d94267fc.html?locale=en-US

Why restricted users can still TECO and remove the user status?

Your current setup block changes by user status but the restricted users can still run BUTA Revoke Technical Completion and change the user status back. The reason behind that is:

  1. In Business Transaction Control, BUTA is the business transaction for Revoke Technical Completion.
  2. Status profile blocking is not user specific. If BUTA is permitted for the order status, then anyone with the authorisation can trigger it.

So, the gap is not your status profile. The gap is that the restricted users still have authorisations that allow status change, and or revoke TECO function in the order app.

Currently you have the following well set up:

  • You used BTAB to activate blocking at TECO
  • You used BUTA to revoke

As next you should split roles by “TECO revoke allowed” vs “TECO revoke forbidden”. For this go to your Maintain Business Roles:

  1. Identify the business catalogs that provide the production order change app and status action. That is, catalogs that contain “Change Production Order” and actions relating to it. See KBA 3611631
  2. Create tow roles. One that can change orders and can revoke TECO and another that can change orders but cannot revoke TECO.

Sometimes TECO and revoke TECO are delivered inside the same app authorisation bundle. In that case, you may not be able to remove on “revoke TECO” while keeping all other change functions in the exact same app for the same order type. Should that limit become reality, I suggest you consider working around it by:

  • Restriction users work in “display” or limited change apps
  • Only a small controller group gets the full change app including revoke TECO

Finally, if your restricted users should not be able to manually change the user status, remove the authorisation for status management from their role. Practically, in Public Cloud you can do this by removing the catalog that grants the capability or tightening the authorisation restrictions within the role.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Validation of Standard SAP TRM Interest Calculation vs. Daily Compounding
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I have some question regarding interest calculation in TRM and would appreciate your insight. 

We are validating interest calculation behavior in SAP S/4HANA TRM (Interest Rate Instruments).

Observed behavior:

  • SAP calculates interest only on the contractual principal.
  • Prior‑day accrued interest is not included in the interest calculation base unless there is an explicit principal increase / capitalization event.
  • This results in linear accrual, not true daily compounding.

Comparison:

SAP: Principal × Daily Rate × (n/360)
Lender: (Principal + Prior‑Day Accrued Interest) × Daily Rate × (n/360)

Questions:

  1. Is it correct that standard SAP TRM has no configuration option (IRI / interest calc method / condition setup) to automatically include prior‑day accrued interest in the interest base?
  2. Is SAP’s design intent to exclude accrued interest from the base for valuation and accounting consistency?
  3. When lender invoices use daily compounding, is the recommended best practice to post the variance as a P&L adjustment at settlement, rather than altering daily accruals?
  4. Would true daily capitalization require custom development or manual principal adjustments (and is that generally discouraged)?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Warehouse Order Confirmation & PGI API
entry.content:
Dear SAP Experts,

  We have successfully integrated SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Embedded EWM with an external system and are able to create Warehouse Tasks (WT) in the background using the API API_WAREHOUSE_ORDER_TASK_2, which is working as expected. However, we require further guidance and the correct APIs to complete the subsequent steps in the process, specifically to confirm the Warehouse Order (WO) or related Warehouse Tasks in the background and then post the Goods Issue (PGI) automatically via API, so that the outbound delivery process can be fully completed without manual intervention.

Currently, the WT creation is successful, but we need the appropriate API names, endpoints, and payload structure for (1) Warehouse Order / Warehouse Task confirmation and (2) Goods Issue posting, along with any prerequisite configuration required in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to ensure seamless end-to-end automation of the warehouse execution process. 

Kindly provide the API'S warehouse order confirmation & through warehouse monitor Fiori App using do the Goods Issue Posting.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Warehouse Order Confirmation & PGI API
entry.content:
For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Embedded EWM, the APIs and documentation for warehouse execution and outbound delivery processing are available in the official SAP Help Portal and SAP API Business Hub.

1. Warehouse Task / Warehouse Order APIs

The API used to create, read and confirm warehouse tasks is:

  • Warehouse Order and Task (A2X) API

Official documentation:

Operations for Warehouse Order and Task API

This API provides operations for:

  • Creating Warehouse Tasks

  • Reading Warehouse Orders and Tasks

  • Confirming Warehouse Tasks

  • Cancelling Warehouse Tasks

As described in the documentation, Warehouse Orders themselves are not confirmed directly via API. The Warehouse Order is automatically completed when all related Warehouse Tasks are confirmed.

 

2. Goods Issue Posting for Outbound Delivery

After the picking warehouse tasks are confirmed, the Goods Issue must be posted on the Outbound Delivery.

The supported API is:

  • Outbound Delivery (A2X) API

Official documentation:

Operations for Warehouse Outbound Delivery Order API

This API supports the Post Goods Issue action for outbound deliveries once the warehouse execution steps are completed.

Any follow up question or further clarification, we remain at your service here!

 

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: ‌🇫🇷‌ French SAP User Group Meeting
entry.content:
The French SAP User Group invites SAP customers and partners to a remote meeting dedicated to recent and upcoming developments in Human Capital Management France. This session provides a structured update on the product roadmap, regulatory topics, and operational feedback, with a strong focus on payroll and DSN-related changes.

The meeting is intended for SAP professionals involved in HCM France who are looking to stay informed about current decisions from French institutions, ongoing transitions, and practical lessons learned.

Agenda

  1. Presentation of development plan HCM France for the last 6 months and the next 6 months by PO HCM France
  2. “Fait générateur” (use contribution rates and thresholds of the for-period for active employees) : Information on the latest decisions from the Institutions for a productive transition in 2027
  3. DSN transition to P26V01 : Incident Review
  4. DSN Reminder and Substitution
  5. Update of Work Zone HCM France
  6. Q/A 
This meeting offers the opportunity to gain a consolidated view of recent developments, better understand upcoming changes, and exchange directly on open questions with SAP and the user community.

The event will be conducted in French.

REGISTER
 
Want to connect with others and learn more about HCM in France? Join the France - Human Capital Management Localization Work Zone group, where SAP experts share updates and guidance.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA 2025 and FPS01: What’s New and Why It Matters
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA 2025 continues SAP’s strategy of delivering innovation with stability. The release introduces targeted functional enhancements across cross‑application components and core business areas, while preserving compatibility and minimizing disruption for on‑premise and private cloud customers. SAP’s structured “What’s New” approach enables organizations to clearly identify relevant changes and assess business value efficiently.

The first Feature Package Stack (FPS01) builds on the initial release and enhances areas such as analytics, master data governance, value chain monitoring or asset management The update is designed for low adoption risk and requires significantly less effort than a full release upgrade. The release also expands intelligent capabilities, including Joule-enabled scenarios, embedding AI-driven insights and automation where they deliver concrete business outcomes and align with enterprise governance.

Overall, SAP S/4HANA 2025 and FPS01 offer a mature, stable innovation path—providing transparency, incremental improvements, and confidence for both upgrade planning and longterm roadmaps. The message is clear: progress without disruption.

Want to learn more, follow the link:

Whats new viewer

entry.title: Extract F.01 output into internal table
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I need to extract the output of F.01 into an internal table and expose it via RFC, trying submit is giving me unstructured data and I am unable to extract it into a table. I see transaction S_ALR_87012284 is same as F.01 but it seems like it is not generated from report writer to be extract via CRIF_RW_WEB_CALL_REPORT.  I'm on ECC6.

Is there any other standard FM to extract? Any help is much appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: What is Agentic Memory?
entry.content:
Agentic memory is a hot topic in the AI industry: big, B2C services like ChatGPT now have memory capabilities, popular agent development frameworks like langgraph define interfaces for memory interactions, and out-of-the-box memory solutions like mem0 provide plug-and-play memory capabilities to AI agents.
 
Why is that? From a 10,000-foot perspective, we can quickly state that agentic memory is about agents "remembering" past interactions to perform better in the future. Without memory, AI systems suffer amnesia, starting blank with every interaction.

But when exactly do agentic systems need memory? What are the business use (and do not use) cases? How does agentic memory differ from other types of memory, and how can we define it? This is what this article is about.
 

When to Use Agentic Memory?

To better reason about when to use agentic memory, I've found it helpful to start from first principles. My initial approach is to examine the types of information that should and should not be stored in agentic memory. The following considerations act as a thought experiment: a strong case for agentic memory begins to emerge when the information to be stored within agentic memory meets the following criteria and avoids the listed counterexamples:
 
 
First Principles Perspective: What to store in agentic memory and what not.
The information is...
Counterexample
Valuable across multiple interactions, carrying significance beyond a single session.A one-off query for a customer's order status. This fact should be retrieved via a live API call.
Novel, meaning it is not already contained within or contradicted by the base LLM's pre-trained knowledge.A request for the definition of "SWOT analysis." This is standard business knowledge already known by the model, making memory storage redundant.
Emergent and unspecified, meaning it is unknown at design time and is only discovered through interaction.A company's official list of approved software vendors. This is pre-defined information, best handled by a dedicated retrieval (RAG) system or prompting.
Unavailable from external systems like databases or APIs that could be queried directly.A customer's billing address. This structured, sensitive data must live in and be queried from a secure CRM, not duplicated in the agent's memory.
Constructed by the agent from its inputs, feedback, and self-reflection.None, this is a prerequisite. If this cannot be achieved, memory cannot be built.
 
 
 
Conversely, if any of these conditions are not met, we should consider whether a more conventional tool, such as a direct database query or a standard RAG system, would be a better solution.
In contrast, the following scenarios are prime examples of situations in which agentic memory is not just helpful but often necessary for an agent to perform its tasks effectively.
 
 
Use Cases for Agentic Memory
Use CaseDescriptionExample
PersonalizationTailor the agent's workflow, communication style, or outputs to the needs, preferences, and history of a specific user.A customer service agent remembers a user's technical expertise level and provides advanced troubleshooting steps instead of basic ones.
Agile ContextualizationProvide the agent with narrow-scope, limited-lifespan information to be used across multiple interactions within a specific project, case, or workflow.An agent helping a team with a two-week software remembers conversations, decisions, and code snippets related only to that sprint.
Extended Working MemoryProvide an agent with relevant facts from a long history of interactions and tool use, overcoming the fixed-size limitations of an LLM's context window.During a long-running legal case analysis, the agent pulls key precedents from conversations held months ago, even if they exceed the context window.
Multi-Agent OrchestrationServe as a shared knowledge exchange layer, or "blackboard," for multiple agents to coordinate their work on a mutual goal.In a research team, one agent finds a key statistic and saves it to shared memory, allowing another agent to incorporate it into the final report immediately.
Curation of Latent KnowledgeExtract and make persistent the undocumented "tribal knowledge" of experts on business procedures or semantics from their interactions with the agent.By working with a senior engineer through a system diagnostics case, a support agent learns an undocumented process and saves it for future use.
Agentic Self-ImprovementAllow an agent to reflect on its own performance and environmental feedback to refine its approach for future tasks systematically.After a user marks a summary as "unhelpful," the agent analyzes the feedback and adjusts its summarization strategy to be more detailed next time.
 
 
 
These use cases — from personalization to self-improvement — suggest that agentic memory behaves more like human memory than computer databases. Let’s explore that distinction to arrive at a clear definition.

 

Trying to Define "Agentic Memory"

To delineate Agentic Memory further, I find it useful to examine its relationship to the concepts of Computer Memory and Human Memory. Here is a glimpse at my current point of view on these terms and their characteristics:
 
Computer Memory is often described and discussed through the technical lens of read-write speeds, size, volatility, scalability, and cost. These are also the dimensions used to cluster and characterize types of computer memory (e.g., cache vs. RAM=memory vs. secondary storage, vs. cloud storage). In computer science, the term is driven by "how" to store things. "What" to do with the memory is often discussed within the context of files, file systems, and databases. In computer science, memory is closely tied to the underlying substrate.
 
Based on my intuitive understanding (not a neuroscientist!) of Human Memory, I tend to discuss it from the perspective of "what" or through the lens of its lifecycle phases and big-picture purpose ("why"). Terms like certainty, connection, validity (e.g., time, scope), importance, attention, meaning, or interpretation come to mind. The lines between different types of memory, such as long-term and short-term, are less distinct than in computer memory. I also strongly associate human memory with learning mechanisms like self-reflection. E.g., humans start to actively search for confirmation of memories after a certain amount of time, and they constantly reconnect and restructure memories. Thus, my understanding of human memory also assumes a natural connection to learning.
 
I know of no singular, widely accepted definition for Agentic Memory. Defining it is a matter of perspective and emphasis. From my point of view, I find it helpful to employ a working definition closer to my perception of human memory, focusing on "why", "what", and lifecycle aspects instead of the memory substrate:
 
 
 
Agentic Memory is a system designed to manage agent-specific, emergent information that holds persistent value. It provides this service to an AI agent by managing the complete memory lifecycle:
  • Formation – Capturing novel insights from interactions and experiences.
  • Evolution – Refining, synthesizing, and connecting stored knowledge through reflection.
  • Retrieval – Intelligently surfacing relevant information to enhance the agent's context and decision-making for future tasks.
 
 
 
Memory evolution may not just involve memory updates but also strategic consolidation, optimization, rephrasing, interpretation, and hierarchization of memories aimed at improving agentic performance. In contrast to memory formation and retrieval, I consider it optional.
 
But even without memory evolution, I do not see agentic memory without learning! Instead, the very essence of engineering agentic memory systems is to define the principles and guidelines that govern the formation (and evolution) of memories and to provide a suitable substrate for storing and retrieving the anticipated information.
 

What's Next?

In my next blog post, I will share a summary of core agentic memory concepts, including a deep dive into memory lifecycle phases, an overview of different memory types, and memory architectures. Stay tuned and feel free to share your thoughts on this point of view. 
 
 
# CTO Research and Innovation Munich
@ladenaw

entry.title: SAP 2026: Between standard power and individual competitive advantage
entry.content:
The world of SAP SE is changing. While SAP stock and the SAP dividend dominate the headlines of the financial press, companies are facing a much deeper question: How much standardization is necessary and how much individuality is permissible?

At Toraman Consulting, we follow a clear principle: The SAP standard is the foundation, but we don't condemn in-house developments . It's about maintaining a balance.

The foundation: Why the standard is more important today than ever

With the rise of SAP Cloud and innovations like Joule (SAP's new AI assistant), a clean core is becoming essential. Those who are too deeply entrenched in the system code of the old SAP R/3 world cannot fully leverage the advantages of S/4HANA and the new Joule agents . The standard ensures stability, update capability, and access to state-of-the-art functions in modules such as SAP MM , SAP SD , and SAP PP .

The optional part: Where in-house developments make the difference

But we also know that a company isn't successful simply by doing the exact same thing as everyone else. Real competitive advantages often lie in processes that are anything but run-of-the-mill.

Our philosophy at Toraman Consulting :

  • Weighing options instead of rejecting: We carefully examine where the standard is sufficient and where a targeted extension provides the decisive added value.
  • Securing advantages: If an in-house development strengthens your market position or makes your customers happier, then we will defend this solution.
  • Modern Development: We use the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to create individuality without contaminating the digital core.

Partnerships and Market Trends 2026

The market is responding to this need for expert knowledge. Large consulting firms like KPMG and Deloitte are heavily investing in strategic alliances to bridge the gap between standard software and customized AI solutions. As a partner in the SAP Open Ecosystem, we offer you precisely this flexibility: the security of a global market leader combined with the personalized support your business requires.

Conclusion: Balance wins

Whether you're building your SAP systems from scratch or need a complex migration strategy, let's discuss your options together. The goal is a system that's stable enough for the future, yet flexible enough to reflect your unique strengths.

entry.title: Update the floating license software for PowerDesigner 16.7
entry.content:
Hello,

We have PowerDesigner 16.7 with floating license management.
The license management executable is on a dedicated machine that needs to be migrated (Windows Server 2016 to 2022).
As part of this process, we would also like to update the licensing software, which appears to be FlexNet Licensing (v11.14.0.0 build 183228 x64_n6).

What would be the procedure to follow?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Update the floating license software for PowerDesigne...
entry.content:
Hello,

PowerDesigner 16.7 with floating-type licenses indeed requires the installation of SySAM 2.4 or preferably 2.4 SP02.
KBA 3583186 specifies the SySAM upgrade requirement due to FlexNet Publisher 11.16.2 reaching end of life on December 20, 2024, and notes SySAM 2.4 SP02 uses FlexNet v11.16.2.0 build 242433

The SySAM 2.4 SP02 requires the Visual C++ Redistributable Package for Visual Studio 2013.
Since this redistributable reached end-of-support on April 9, 2024, SySAM 2.5.0 was released and is now included with PowerDesigner 16.7 SP09.

To download the software, you need to follow the steps listed below:
1. Connect to SAP Software Downloads
https://me.sap.com/softwarecenter/search/powerdesigner
2. Select 'SAP POWERDESIGNER 16.7 - Maintenance Product'.
3. Select 'SAP POWERDESIGNER 16.7'.
4. The screen is refreshed.
Click POWDES167009_0-80005353.ZIP to download the software PowerDesigner 16.7 SP09.
The file contains PowerDesigner_16.7.SP09.8174.exe, PowerDesigner_16.7.SP09.8174_DVD.zip, PowerDesigner_16.7.SP09.8174_Portal.16915.exe, PowerDesigner_16.7.SP09.8174_Proxy.exe, SQLAnywhere 17.0 SP1 PL19 For PowerDesigner 16.7 SP09.zip and SYSAM 2.5.0.zip.

Kind regards

entry.title: RFP Automation with Agentic AI: My Journey from Manual RFPs to Smarter Sourcing
entry.content:
How RFP automation works with SAP’s Sourcing Event Agent  

Keeping up with AI can begin to feel like a part‑time job on top of the one you already have. That’s why the real wins don’t come from chasing the latest model. They come when AI, people, processes, and culture evolve together-and when RFP automation removes grunt work so teams can focus on strategy. Nowhere is that more obvious than in strategic sourcing, where agentic AI is reshaping how we create RFPs, evaluate suppliers, and make decisions that matter. 

A trade show RFP that had me floundering 

Early in my career I led a marketing events team and was asked to produce an RFP for a set of trade show booths and the services that go with them. I’d responded to RFPs before, but I’d never written one. I had no idea what I was in for. I had to research vendors, define requirements, write each question so it could be universally understood, and then reconcile spreadsheet after spreadsheet to score responses. I spent nights trying to make sense of conflicting line items like installation fees that were listed throughout the responses in different places, ambiguous add‑ons, and late vendor clarifications that required another round of follow‑up. I learned in real time what not to do.  

That project taught me three things:

  1. Sourcing events are equal parts detail work and judgment. 
  2. Small omissions, like not asking whether a vendor’s “standard setup” included freight or on‑site labor, can become expensive surprises. 
  3. Much of the manual work added almost no strategic value. I found myself drowning in a process that didn’t support the real decision points.

RFP automation with agentic AI: Why the Sourcing Event Agent has me leaning in 

If I had to create that same RFP today, it would look completely different. Within SAP Ariba Sourcing, I can just tell the Sourcing Event Agent what I need in plain, everyday language, and it will do in minutes the research that once took days. It recommends vendors based on supplier history and trusted market benchmarks, generates standardized RFP questions, and assembles a clean, comparison-based vendor matrix. 

  • Top agentic capabilities  
    • Vendor discovery that uses your supplier history and market data
    • Standardized question generation to improve comparability
    • Bid analysis that flags vague claims and missing compliance answers 
    • Easy tracing of sources for AI-assisted recommendations 
  • Estimated ROI* 
    • ~70% faster sourcing event launches 
    • ~80% faster bid analysis  

Because the Sourcing Event Agent connects to your qualified suppliers and past sourcing events, it drafts RFPs that reflect your environment and recent history. For regulated industries, the agent makes it simple to trace sources for recommendations.  

Moreover, the agent surfaces questions I wouldn’t necessarily think to ask. For example, it will prompt contingency questions such as, “What are your procedures for weather‑related delays?” and “What exactly is included in the standard setup and what triggers extra fees?” Those prompts could have helped my team avoid costly, last-minute surprises. 

Before vs. now 

Before: Now: 
A 100‑page RFP that required days of spreadsheet reconciliation and overtime to pull insights for stakeholders, while trying to hide from my leadership team how messy everything was.  A focused 30- to 40-page RFP generated in hours, with an automatically produced shortlist, and a vendor matrix that lets reviewers compare line items at a glance. 

See Sourcing Event Agent in action 

Speed isn't the only advantage. With AI embedded in the workflow, I can seamlessly access sourcing, supplier, and other data to understand the business context of different decisions. I don’t have to export files into a separate tool and hope for the best. The agent drafts RFPs with my business rules and sourcing history in mind. 

How RFP automation with agentic AI works  

Agentic AI needs the same briefing you’d give a colleague, including insights on the project objective and success criteria, scope and deliverables, timeline and key dates, budget and evaluation criteria, and any compliance constraints. Historical artifacts like previous RFPs, past responses, supplier performance data, and your scoring rubric help align outputs with your team’s needs. 

The amount of autonomy you give AI depends on your comfort level with risk. Risk-tolerant teams may allow the agent to draft full RFP sections and recommend shortlists, while accepting occasional oversights in exchange for speed. Risk-averse teams treat the agent like a smart assistant and review every output before it goes live. 

Most organizations find a middle ground and let the AI handle vendor discovery, market analysis, and initial drafts, while humans make final vendor selections, set evaluation criteria, and handle nuanced trade‑offs. The key is to be really honest when it comes to your organization’s DNA. Do you like to move fast and iterate, or do you prefer to go more slowly and feel certain everything's right the first time? 

Laying the foundation for agentic AI 

Agentic AI doesn't replace human judgment; it amplifies it. By removing repetitive tasks with tools like the Sourcing Event Agent, your procurement team can spend more time aligning strategy with the company’s goals, engaging stakeholders and advising leadership on critical decisions that drive long‑term value.  

Adoption rests on four pillars: 

  1. Clear communication: When you decide it’s time for your team to use AI-assisted solutions, explain why AI is being introduced and how it will change day‑to‑day work for practitioners.  
  2. Training: Show your team where the AI gets its data, how it works, and when to trust or override its recommendations. 
  3. Internal champions: Involve respected practitioners early on. Their endorsements carry more weight than top-down announcements.  
  4. Celebrate and learn: Acknowledge wins and share lessons from failures. Learning together builds trust and momentum.

Getting started: Pilot RFP automation with low-risk tests 

To ease into AI-based RFP automation, choose a low‑risk category like office supplies or basic maintenance, let the agent draft the RFP, and then review everything carefully. Use the process as a learning exercise to understand how the AI reasons and which prompts work best. 

You don’t need a perfectly mature function to start using AI. Basic building blocks like a supplier list (even a spreadsheet), past RFPs, templates, and an approval workflow are enough to get you started. Agentic AI can act like an experienced colleague on demand, surfacing best practices and standardized questions. Plus, it learns with every sourcing event through the feedback you give it. The more you use it and iterate with it, the smarter it becomes, which means future RFPs will require fewer clarifications and drive tighter pricing responses. 

Make it a shared experience by involving as many practitioners as you can. Listen to all of their feedback and make necessary adjustments. Over time this will help you refine your templates, scoring rubrics, and governance so the system learns your organization’s standards. 

Key takeaways 

  • Agentic AI needs the same briefing information you’d give a colleague, such as: objectives, scope, timeline, budget, evaluation criteria, and compliance constraints.
  • Historical artifacts (past RFPs, supplier performance, contracts) improve output.
  • The Sourcing Events Agent automates vendor research, standardizes RFPs tied to SAP data, and surfaces critical questions and risks.
  • Autonomy depends on risk tolerance; most teams use AI for discovery and drafting and keep humans for final judgments. 
  • Successful adoption requires communication, training, internal champions, and shared learning.

Want to learn the basic steps to implement AI in procurement?  

Read our new white paper with the Hackett Group for a practical roadmap and starter playbook. 

*Source: SAP Value Management

entry.title: Re: standard column: Units per Year
entry.content:
Normally bonus amounts are one-time pay components and not recurring and therefore do not have a frequency attached the same way that the recurring pay component used for base salary does.

Can you share more information about how your bonus planning is done? Is it a monthly recurring value? Can you provide a screenshot of the pay component being used for bonus?

Are you calculating bonuses on the salary worksheet and not using variable pay?
